notes: Daily draft 2026-07-09. Sources: Wikipedia lead extracts in data/sourcing/docs/sports_games_leisure/. Pack skews $1000-light per coverage gap. Answers chosen to avoid all in-flight PR content. Cool Papa Bell uses answer_type other because bare surname Bell is too ambiguous. Björn Borg clue approaches from the McEnroe-1981-final angle for $800 obliqueness.

questions.clue: Richard T. James designed this helical spring toy in 1943; it sold out at its Gimbels department store debut in 90 minutes at one dollar apiece.

citations.title: Slinky
citations.snippet: The Slinky is a helical spring toy invented and developed by American naval engineer Richard T. James in 1943 and successfully demonstrated at Gimbels department store in Philadelphia on November 27, 1945.

questions.clue: In sport, this term describes the achievement of a positive feat exactly three times within the same game.

citations.title: Hat-trick
citations.snippet: A hat-trick or hat trick is the achievement of a generally positive feat three times in a match, or another achievement based on the number three.

questions.clue: This Green Bay Packers head coach won three consecutive NFL championships and both of the first two Super Bowls during his seven seasons with the team.

citations.title: Vince Lombardi
citations.snippet: Vincent Thomas Lombardi was an American professional football coach and executive in the National Football League (NFL). Lombardi is considered by many to be among the greatest coaches and leaders in American sports. He is best known as the

questions.clue: This quarterback won a record seven Super Bowl championships over a career split between New England and Tampa Bay.

citations.title: Tom Brady
citations.snippet: Thomas Edward Patrick Brady Jr. is an American former professional football quarterback who played in the National Football League (NFL) for 23 seasons. He spent his first 20 seasons with the New England Patriots and was a central contribut

questions.clue: Released by Namco in 1980, this iconic maze-action arcade game was originally titled 'Puck Man' in Japan before the name was altered for export.

citations.title: Pac-Man
citations.snippet: Pac-Man, originally titled Puck Man in Japan, is a 1980 maze video game developed and published by Namco for arcades. It was released in Japan on May 22, 1980 and by Midway Manufacturing in North America in August 1980. The player controls

questions.clue: At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, this American track-and-field athlete won four gold medals and set Olympic records in every individual event he entered.

citations.title: Jesse Owens
citations.snippet: James Cleveland "Jesse" Owens was an American track and field athlete who made history at the 1936 Olympic Games by winning four gold medals, setting individual Olympic records in each event, plus another as a member of the 400-meter relay.

questions.clue: This 1978 Taito arcade title was the first video game to feature endless gameplay and helped expand the medium from a novelty into a global industry.

citations.title: Space Invaders
citations.snippet: Space Invaders is a 1978 shoot 'em up game developed and published by Taito for arcades. Taito released it in Japan in July 1978 and overseas through a partnership with Midway Manufacturing later that year. Space Invaders was the first vide

questions.clue: Standing 7 feet 1 inch tall, this NBA center played 14 seasons and was elected to the league's 35th, 50th, and 75th anniversary all-time teams.

citations.title: Wilt Chamberlain
citations.snippet: Wilton Norman Chamberlain was an American professional basketball player. Standing 7 feet 1 inch (2.16 m) tall, he played center in the National Basketball Association (NBA) for 14 seasons. He was enshrined in the Naismith Memorial Basketba

questions.clue: World chess champion from 1985 to 2000, this grandmaster held the world number-one ranking for a record 255 months over the course of his career.

citations.title: Garry Kasparov
citations.snippet: Garry Kimovich Kasparov is a Russian chess grandmaster, political activist and writer, who was the World Chess Champion from 1985 to 2000. His peak FIDE chess rating of 2851, achieved in 1999, was the highest recorded until being surpassed

questions.clue: Regarded by historians as one of the fastest men ever to play the game, this Negro Leagues center fielder was active from 1922 to 1946.

citations.title: Cool Papa Bell
citations.snippet: James Thomas "Cool Papa" Bell was an American center fielder and pitcher in Negro league baseball and the Mexican League from 1922 to 1946. He is considered to have been one of the fastest men ever to play the game. He was elected to the Ba

questions.clue: Running barefoot through the streets of Rome in 1960, this Ethiopian became the first person of sub-Saharan African descent to win an Olympic gold medal.

citations.title: Abebe Bikila
citations.snippet: Shambel Abebe Bikila was an Ethiopian marathon runner who was a back-to-back Olympic marathon champion. He was the first Ethiopian Olympic gold medalist, winning his first gold medal at the 1960 Summer Olympics in Rome while running barefoo

questions.clue: No Wimbledon men's champion before or since has matched this Swede's five consecutive titles, a run ended by John McEnroe in the 1981 final.

citations.title: Björn Borg
citations.snippet: Björn Rune Borg is a Swedish former professional tennis player. He was ranked as the world No. 1 in men's singles by the Association of Tennis Professionals (ATP) for 109 weeks. Borg won 66 singles titles during his career, including eleven

questions.clue: This 1968 Olympic gold medalist revolutionized the high jump by clearing the bar in a backward arc, a technique now bearing his surname and used by virtually every top competitor.

citations.title: Dick Fosbury

questions.clue: This hockey star accumulated more career assists alone than any other player's total career points — a feat widely called the most unbreakable record in professional sports.

citations.title: Wayne Gretzky
citations.snippet: Wayne Douglas Gretzky is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player and head coach. He played 20 seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) for four teams from 1979 to 1999. Nicknamed "the Great One", he has been called the greatest

questions.clue: Banned from baseball in 1989 for wagering on his own team's games while managing them, this player still holds the all-time MLB records for career hits, games played, and at-bats.

citations.title: Pete Rose
